notes: 12-microbe Phase 1 catalog. First 6 are canonical DN cast per Docs/HANDOFF_FROM_LABSMITH_DISTRIBUTED_NARRATIVE_RETROFIT.md (Wave 32b). Remaining 6 are codex-supporting microbes (beneficial-first framing per CLAUDE.md trauma-informed posture). Names checked against labsmith REGISTRY_PORTFOLIO_CHARACTER_NAMES.md. v2 adds per-character voiceLines (3-5 sample dialogue lines per DN-S voice-register card).

microbes.catchphrase: Friend in your food. Friend in your gut.
microbes.factCard: Lactobacillus species ferment milk into yogurt and live in your large intestine, where they often help with digestion.
voiceLines: Have you ever tasted yogurt? That's a little bit of me.
I get along with most of my neighbors down here.
Fiber is good — but balanced is good too.

microbes.factCard: Saccharomyces cerevisiae turn sugars into carbon dioxide bubbles — that's what makes bread dough rise.
voiceLines: Give me a little sugar and I'll show you something neat.
Bubbles are just air I've made for you.
Bread, beer, kefir — same family, different parties.

microbes.catchphrase: Sunlight. Then air. Then everything else.
microbes.factCard: Cyanobacteria use sunlight to make their own food and release oxygen. They helped create the oxygen in Earth's atmosphere.
voiceLines: Long before there were trees, there was me.
Sunlight in — oxygen out. That's my whole job.
If you can breathe, you can thank a few billion of us.

microbes.factCard: Mycorrhizal fungi link tree roots underground, often helping plants share water and nutrients across long distances.
voiceLines: Trees share through me — water, sugar, news of insects.
Below the forest is another forest.
Patience is a tool. Use it more often than you think you should.

microbes.catchphrase: Some friends. Some not. All real.
microbes.factCard: Some bacteria can cause illness when they grow too large in number, but most of them live with us peacefully.
voiceLines: I'm not the bad guy of the story.
Most of my family is fine company. A few of us are not.
Names like 'pathogen' are shortcuts — look closer before you decide.

microbes.catchphrase: I check IDs. Patient and careful.
microbes.factCard: Your immune cells — like macrophages and B-cells — patiently check every microbe they meet and learn which ones to trust.
voiceLines: I work slowly because I want to get it right.
Most of who I meet, I wave through with a smile.
Your body knows what's family. I help it remember.

microbes.factCard: Bifidobacterium species feast on the fiber in fruits and vegetables — one of the gut's most helpful families.
voiceLines: Anything fibrous, I love. Apples, beans, broccoli.
We were here before you started eating solid food.
Sugar alone isn't enough for me — I need real fiber.

microbes.factCard: Akkermansia muciniphila feeds on the gut's mucus layer and helps keep the intestinal wall strong.
voiceLines: Mucus isn't gross — it's how I work.
Strong walls keep everything where it belongs.
I'm small but I do a lot of repair.

microbes.factCard: Many Streptococcus species live quietly in the mouth and throat — a few can cause illness, but most are part of normal mouth life.
voiceLines: I'm usually invisible to you. That's the goal.
We get loud sometimes, but most of us prefer quiet.
Brush your teeth — it makes both our lives easier.

microbes.catchphrase: Most of us help. A few don't.
microbes.factCard: Most Escherichia coli strains live peacefully in your gut and help make vitamin K. Only a few specific strains can make people sick.
voiceLines: We make vitamin K. You needed it for your bones today.
When the news says 'E. coli,' that's a few cousins of mine — not all of us.
I'm one of the most-studied microbes on Earth.

microbes.catchphrase: I am very small. I prefer noses.
microbes.factCard: Rhinoviruses are tiny viruses that often cause the common cold. They are the most common reason kids sniffle in winter.
voiceLines: I'm only here for a few days, usually.
Cold weather doesn't make me stronger — people just stay closer together.
Wash your hands. It really does send me away.

microbes.catchphrase: I survive what nothing else can.
microbes.factCard: Deinococcus radiodurans is an extremophile that survives radiation, cold, and dehydration that would defeat most life on Earth.
voiceLines: Tough doesn't mean unfriendly.
Dry me out — I'll wait. Days, years.
Scientists keep poking at me to learn how I bounce back.
